content:
5 min read
Just now

--

A few years ago I blew $22,000 on AWS in a single month (10x my budget). The budget had been flat for over a year — and I just stopped checking. During this complacency a runaway process started firing IoT events in a loop running for weeks before the invoice caught it. The data was in Cost Explorer the whole time. I just wasn’t looking.
That’s the real problem with cloud cost — not missing data, missing attention. The console is a place you have to remember to visit, and you stop visiting the moment things feel stable. Enter the Cost Guardian!
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Why I built it
Recently I wanted to watch spend on a new project, so I looked at what AWS ships before building anything. There are two pieces to it. Cost Anomaly Detection handles the alerting — it’s free, machine-learning based, and routes to Slack. Then there’s the AI layer, Amazon Q Developer in chat (the tool that used to be AWS Chatbot), which lets you ask “why did costs jump last week?” in plain language. That layer was the part I actually wanted, and it’s where I hit a wall: the free tier covers 25 account-context questions a month, and past that it needs an Amazon Q Developer Pro subscription at $19 per user, per month.
The alerting on its own wasn’t enough. A raw anomaly alert tells you a number moved and then stops — it can’t say why, it has no idea what your services are for, and there’s nothing there to reply to or correct. The conversational analysis that fills that gap is the piece behind the paywall, and it’s priced per seat, so every teammate who wanted to see it added another $19 a month. For a lot of projects, the math doesn’t work for me.
So I vibe-coded my own over a few evenings: slack-aws-cost-guardian. Same shape — anomaly alerts plus a plain-language “why” in Slack, and a bot you can actually talk to — but the AI runs on whatever LLM I point it at, priced only by how much I chat with it, and there’s no per-seat fee. Here’s the honest comparison:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Two of those rows are the actual reason it exists. Because it’s open source, it isn’t tied to AWS at all — I built it to fold in other cost sources the same way: LLM API bills, Databricks, Azure, anything with a billing endpoint. That’s the real cost surface of a hybrid stack, and the native tool only ever sees the AWS slice. And because it carries a context file plus a learned memory, it gets more useful the longer it runs instead of answering every question cold.
The cost row is narrower than it looks: below 25 questions a month, AWS’s free tier already covers you, and at real team scale the managed, compliance-ready option is worth paying for. The place the cost guardian fits is in the middle — past the free questions, but nowhere near filling a $19-a-seat subscription — where a few cents of tokens does the job and I get to choose the model.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

What it does

• Scheduled Lambda pulls Cost Explorer into DynamoDB for history.

• Anomaly detection on an exponentially-weighted 14-day baseline, z-score triggered; budget alerts at 80% and 100%.

• Daily and weekly summaries, so you see spend even when nothing’s wrong.

• An LLM (Claude or GPT) writes the why in plain language, not just that a number moved.

• Alert buttons — mark a cost expected or unexpected, and it learns from that.

• An @mention bot answers in-thread: @guardian what did we spend yesterday?
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Where the AI earns its place
Two choices worth calling out. First, the model doesn’t do detection — a z-score against a rolling baseline does that fine, and you shouldn’t pay an LLM to compute one. The model translates. A raw alert says “data transfer out up $220 this week.” The model says “data transfer out up $220, almost all from the prod NAT gateway — matches the image service you shipped Tuesday, which is routing S3 through the gateway instead of a VPC endpoint.” The second version tells you what to go fix. That leap only works if it knows what your services are, so it reads a guardian-context.md describing your accounts and normal patterns before it analyzes.
Second, it learns. Mark an alert “expected” and a small curator folds that into a memory it carries into every future check — tell it once the Sunday-night backup spike is normal and it stops flagging it. The memory is plain files, no vector database — at this scale you don’t need one, and skipping it keeps the whole thing legible and cheap to run.
Low noise on purpose
I learned long ago… noisy monitoring alerts are useless. People learn to ignore them.
So: daily summary by default, thresholds in real dollars, and a loop that gets quieter the more you correct it. It should earn every interruption.
Democratizing FinOps
Cost usually lives behind a billing console and an IAM role, which makes it one person’s job — the person with the access. Everyone else finds out secondhand, if at all. Putting the numbers in Slack changes who’s in the room: a founder, someone in finance, an engineer who’ll never open the billing console, all looking at the same daily figure. That’s what I mean by democratizing FinOps — cost becomes the whole team’s to watch, without everyone needing access to the account.
Take it
It’s MIT-licensed. Fork it, or point your coding assistant at the repo and lift what’s useful. It’s a worked example, not a product — the shape is the point, and the shape is easy to copy.
https://github.com/danjamk/slack-aws-cost-guardian
Where this goes
I’m pretty happy where it ended up. Part of me wants to take it farther. Maybe adding system alerts from the applications. Maybe adding more context such as access to source code, application logs, etc. Again… with the idea of democratizing observability and knowledge. But I may chase another shiny object instead.
If it seems to fit your needs, I hope it gives some value. Share any comments or even contribute if you are motivated.
